narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, LSAT score of 34, middle-family income background, part-time enrollment plans, and school decile rankings of 5th in Year 1 and 4th in Year 3, which reflect solid but not top-tier academic performance so far. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 52.9% chance that you would fail it on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile that most strongly shaped the prediction. The biggest influence was your choice to attend part-time, which uncommonly applies to only about 7% of students and pushed the model toward seeing a higher risk of bar failure, likely due to patterns in how part-time schedules correlate with exam outcomes in past data. On a more positive note, your LSAT score was the second-strongest factor, pulling the prediction toward success despite being a bit below the typical 36.7 average—showing it still demonstrated real potential. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA ranked third in influence, also nudging things favorably as it exceeds the dataset average of 3.21, highlighting your college-level strengths. Compared to typical students, your grades place you mid-pack in your school's deciles, which didn't sway the outcome as much here. While these positives helped, they weren't enough to offset the part-time concern in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your abilities or background—many strong candidates face similar hurdles. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time plan if feasible, boosting your LSAT through targeted prep, or strengthening other experiences like internships to build a fuller picture. You might also retake courses to climb those school deciles or explore schools with more flexible models for part-time students. With your solid foundation, targeted improvements can shift the odds in your favor next time. Keep pushing—you've got the grit to make this happen.
